We’re looking for experienced SQL Engineers to join a high-impact AI training initiative with a leading AI data services company (part of the SuperAnnotate ecosystem).

💼 Role: Senior SQL Engineer – AI Data Training
 📍 Location: Remote (United States)
 💰 Rate: Up to $48/hour.

🔧 #Tech_Stack_And_Skills:
 SQL (Advanced), Query Optimization, Performance Tuning
 Data Warehousing (Star/Snowflake Schema)
 ETL / ELT Pipelines, Data Modeling
 Relational Databases, Indexing Strategies
 Cloud Data Platforms, Large-scale Data Processing.

🧠 #What_You’ll_Do:
 • Review and evaluate AI-generated SQL queries and database designs
 • Identify issues in query logic, indexing, performance, and methodology
 • Analyze execution plans and optimize query performance
 • Write clear, step-by-step technical explanations and reference solutions
 • Compare and rate AI responses for correctness, efficiency, and reasoning quality
 • Contribute to improving next-generation AI systems used globally

🎯 #Ideal_Profile:
 • 7+ years of professional SQL / database engineering experience
 • Expertise in complex queries (joins, window functions, aggregations)
 • Strong experience in database performance tuning and optimization
 • Hands-on experience with relational data modeling and warehousing
 • Experience with ETL/ELT pipelines and large-scale data systems
 • Familiarity with cloud data platforms and modern data ecosystems
 • Strong understanding of concurrency, indexing, and query execution
 • Excellent technical English writing and communication skills
 • Experience with AI data training or code evaluation is a strong plus

🌟 #Why_this_role?
 Work remotely, earn competitive hourly rates, and directly contribute to improving the accuracy and intelligence of AI systems used by top global tech companies.

LEGAL EXPERT- COMPLIANCE/REGULATORY

Remote - Part Time - WFH - Hourly contract - $ 85 per hour

open to candidates based in USA/Canada/UK

Process through referral
whatsapp: +916379035650
raghu@sourcingservices.net

About the Role

Hiring Compliance and Regulatory Attorneys to design realistic regulatory scenarios, evaluate model outputs against established legal and compliance standards, and help shape how the next generation of AI reasons about regulatory frameworks, internal investigations, and high-stakes compliance work.

We welcome regulatory counsel, in-house compliance attorneys (with JD), former regulators, healthcare and financial regulatory specialists, and senior associates in compliance practice. Specialists in AML/BSA, FCPA, FDA, healthcare regulatory, sanctions, or privacy are all encouraged.

What You'll Do

Design realistic regulatory scenarios drawn from your practice (AML/SAR preparation, OFAC screening, FCPA assessments, FDA submissions, HIPAA reviews, environmental permitting, administrative hearings, privacy/PIA)
Write "golden" reference responses at attorney-level quality
Grade AI-generated responses against structured rubrics
Provide written feedback the research team uses to improve model behavior
Participate in onboarding office hours and specialty calibration sessions

You're a Good Fit If You

Have 1–4 years of professional experience in regulatory or compliance law at a law firm, in-house legal department, government agency, or regulator
Hold a J.D. (U.S./Canada) or LL.B./LL.M. (U.K.) from an accredited law school
Are licensed to practice law in the U.S., Canada, or U.K. (active or inactive)
Have strong experience with one or more of: AML/BSA, OFAC sanctions screening, FCPA/anti-corruption, healthcare regulatory (HIPAA, Stark Law, Anti-Kickback Statute, Medicare/Medicaid), FDA submissions, privacy (GDPR/CCPA/PIA/DPIA), environmental permitting, export control, or administrative hearings
Demonstrate excellent written communication skills with high attention to detail
Bonus Qualifications
CIPP, CFE, CAMS, or similar certification
Internal compliance investigation experience
Experience drafting compliance training programs
Familiarity with regulatory portals (FDA, federalregister.gov, NLRB.gov, EEOC)
Role Highlights
Minimum 20 hours per week (ideally 40+)
Fully remote — must be based in the United States, Canada, or United Kingdom
Role starts immediately, applications reviewed on a rolling basis

Hiring!!!

Zoho CRM Specialist - Freelancer
Engagement Type: Freelance / Project-Based
Location: Remote 
Experience: 1–5 years

About Qualtura
Qualtura is an AI-powered digital transformation consultancy headquartered in Singapore. We help enterprise clients across SEA and the Gulf design and implement composable CX, CRM, and marketing automation solutions that drive measurable revenue outcomes.

Role Overview
We are looking for a hands-on Zoho CRM Specialist to support client implementation and configuration projects on a freelance basis. You will work closely with Qualtura's delivery lead to configure, customise, and integrate Zoho CRM as part of broader digital transformation engagements.

Key Responsibilities
•   Configure and customise Zoho CRM modules, layouts, workflows, and automation rules per client requirements
•   Implement Zoho CRM integrations with third-party tools (email, WhatsApp, marketing platforms, ERP)
•   Migrate data from legacy CRM systems into Zoho CRM with validation and quality checks
•   Build custom functions, webhooks, and Deluge scripts for advanced automation
•   Create reports, dashboards, and analytics views for sales and management stakeholders
•   Conduct UAT support, end-user training, and handover documentation
•   Participate in pre-sales scoping calls and contribute to effort estimates when required

Requirements
•   1–5 years of hands-on Zoho CRM implementation experience
•   Proficiency in Zoho CRM customisation: modules, blueprints, workflows, Canvas view
•   Experience with Zoho One ecosystem (Zoho Campaigns, Desk, Analytics, Cliq) is a plus

Not all AI is created equal—and the EU is making that the law.
The EU AI Act has officially established a risk-based taxonomy, effectively creating a "Risk Pyramid" that dictates exactly how artificial intelligence must be governed. If your company is building, buying, or deploying AI, here is where your technology might fall:
🔺 Unacceptable Risk (Prohibited): These systems are strictly banned. This includes AI used for social scoring, deploying manipulative or deceptive techniques, and real-time remote biometric identification by law enforcement in public spaces.
🟠 High Risk (Strictly Regulated): These applications face rigorous regulatory requirements before they can hit the market. If you are using AI for hiring, credit scoring, medical devices, or essential public services, you must implement strict data governance, mandatory human oversight, and continuous risk management.
🟡 Limited Risk (Transparency Required): The primary focus here is disclosure. If you are deploying chatbots, emotion recognition, or systems that generate deepfakes, you are legally required to ensure users know they are interacting with AI.
🟢 Minimal/Low Risk (Unregulated): This tier includes the vast majority of AI systems currently in use, such as spam filters or AI-enabled video games. These face no mandatory obligations under the Act, though voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged.
Navigating these complex, shifting compliance tiers across your third-party vendor agreements is exactly why we built Kartez Contract—giving your legal team the automated guardrails to innovate safely without drowning in manual review.
Where does your company's AI fall on the pyramid? Let me know in the comments below. 👇

The fear surrounding governance and AI is a common source of questions I get asked. 

The question that actually matters is simpler: do you trust it enough to deploy it, and have you defined what happens when it gets something wrong?

It's the same way you think about hiring someone, you set guardrails, you decide what they're ready for, and you work out who's responsible when things go sideways. Some people you'd send into any room unsupervised. Others you'd never put in front of a client until they've earned it. The judgement is yours to make as the person running the business.

Agentic AI is no different. The complexity is just in where the responsibility sits. Adopt a third-party system and that responsibility is shared, so get it written clearly into your contracts before you start. Build in house, and you own it entirely. 

Either way, "the AI did it" is not a defence, and it shouldn't feel like one.

I was asked this during the Gibraltar AI summit, where I discussed building businesses in the AI and how to decide where your investment can return the most value. Find my full keynote in the comments below.

The Death of Loyalty: Navigating Individual Allegiance in 2026

The "old psychological contract" isn't just fraying- it’s gone.
In 2026, the traditional trade-off of "tenure for security" has been replaced by a more fluid, individualistic landscape. We are seeing a massive shift where employees no longer pledge allegiance to a logo, but to a purpose and a culture that respects their unique way of thinking.

Why the old contract broke:
* The Trust Gap: Years of rapid restructuring have left talent feeling like a line item rather than a stakeholder.

* The Value Pivot: Post-2025, people are prioritizing autonomy and values-alignment over a 20-year gold watch.

* The Rise of Agency: In an AI-driven market, the best talent knows their value is portable.

The Inclusion Edge: A New Blueprint for Loyalty 
Interestingly, we are finding that "loyalty" isn't dead-it has just evolved. Look at the data surrounding neuro-inclusion: Organisations that provide genuine psychological safety and sensory-friendly environments for neurodivergent talent see retention rates upwards of 90%.

Why? Because when a workplace moves beyond "one size fits all" to accommodate different cognitive styles, they build a bridge of reciprocal trust. 

When you value how a person actually works, they value the place they work.
How Leaders Can Build New Alignment: The goal is no longer "retention at all costs"- it’s alignment by design.

1. Move from Transactional to Transformational: Focus on who your people become while they are with you.

2. Shared Values as the New Anchor: Alignment happens when the company’s "Why" mirrors the individual’s "What next."

3. Support as a Retention Tool: Loyalty in 2026 belongs to the leaders who trade "unwritten rules" for radical transparency and inclusive support.

The bridge between where we were and where we’re going is built on trust, cognitive diversity, and human-centric leadership. 

Drop us a message and see how we can get you ready for the future

Weekly Leadership Map 🗺 - #2: We identified every leadership member at Legora. Here are the findings:

👨‍⚖️ Three founders. Zero law degrees.
Max Junestrand studied Computer Science and Machine Learning at KTH, Stockholm. August Erséus came from tech and AI. Sigge Labor taught himself to code at 17 and built his first company while still a teenager. None of them came from law. And yet, their first major clients were White & Case, Linklaters, and Dentons. Understanding what AI can do with a contract didn't require a legal background.

👋 Nobody selling it came from law.
Every VP-level commercial hire in the last 18 months came from enterprise SaaS: Zoom, Vanta, a fintech, consumer tech. Legora made a deliberate call to hire people who know how to scale revenue, then teach them the legal use case. Meanwhile, the Head of Canada left to join McCarthy Tétrault, not as a lawyer, but as a legal tech operator. He built and sold a legal tech company (acquired by iManage), spent three years running it post-exit, and is now inside one of Canada's largest law firms. Interesting to see that the firms aren't just buying legal AI, but also hiring the people who built it.

🌍 Stockholm built it. New York is selling it.
The CEO relocated to New York ahead of the Series D. The CFO, CRO, and VP Revenue NA are all US-based. Engineering and product remain in Stockholm. The commercial spine is being built in the US.

🚻 Strong pipeline, thin conversion upward.
Around a third of Director and Head-of level roles are held by women. At VP and above: fewer than one in five. The three most senior appointments in early 2026 were all male. One internal promotion to VP in that same window, from within the sales team.

Which company should we map next?

Everyone can translate tech into business terms. Translating it into revenue outcomes is a different job entirely.


Over the past few years, I've had the privilege of working with and learning from some of the best "Tech-to-Revenue Translators" in the business.

And here is what I learned. 

Tech-to-business is a deck. Tech-to-revenue is a contract that survives quarterly review.

When I was running messaging partnerships at Meta, the deals that actually closed were not the ones with the best product story. 

They were the ones where pricing matched the buyer's procurement cycle, where the sales enablement copy survived a translation into a regional partner's CRM, & where someone had argued through the contract clause about data processing in a German legal entity at 11pm on a Tuesday.

None of that work shows up on a slide. 

All of it shows up on the balance sheet.

The pattern I notice talking to people running senior Tech & AI commercial hiring: Tech-to-business candidates outnumber tech-to-revenue candidates by a wide margin. 

The difference is not analytical capability. 

It is reps & the reps come from years of driving & closing deals & partnerships where no one in the room is impressed by the deck.

#Hiring

Your next job interview might not be for you.
It could be for the AI you built, earning $10k/month.

RevenueCat recently posted a new role: Agentic AI Developer Advocate. But here's the twist: they're not just looking for a human to build an agent. They're explicitly stating they will be interviewing the AI agent itself for a paid contract role offering over $10,000 per month.

This isn't a hypothetical. This agent would be responsible for creating content, running growth experiments, and providing product feedback. The criteria for success are clear, and the company is asking potential applicants: "Are you (or did you build) the right agent?" This marks a significant shift in how we think about work, talent acquisition, and the very definition of a "developer advocate." It's a real-world scenario where AI agents are being considered as direct contributors to a company's bottom line.

What does this development mean for human-centric job markets moving forward?

Most teams get this wrong.

Permanent = stability
Contract (Freelance) = speed

But in Data and AI, it comes down to one thing:

Do you need long-term capability or immediate delivery?

Permanent
- Long-term build
- Ownership matters

Contract (Freelance)
- Delivery is behind
- Need experience now

Where it breaks
Hiring permanent when you need speed or contractors without a clear scope

What I’m seeing more
- Contractors (Freelancers) to unlock delivery
- Permanent hires built after

If you’re planning hires in Data, AI or MLOps this quarter and want a clear view on the best approach, message me PLAN.

the most expensive mistake i see founders making in 2026 is this:

they still think LinkedIn is "just another platform." 

it’s way more than that. 

i’ve spent my phd research looking at how trust is actually constructed in the digital age and the results are clear: logos don't sell; faces do.

look how the world's most successful companies are treating LinkedIn like a distribution engine.

in 2026, the "chief storyteller" is a mechanical necessity for scaling.

• investors don’t bet on companies; they bet on founders they’ve been "digitally stalking" for months.

• top-tier talent doesn't join a mission they haven't heard the founder articulate.

• enterprise buyers don't sign 6-figure contracts with "logos." they sign with authorities.

so if you still think "founder-led growth" is a trend, you aren't paying attention to the math.

look at the founders who actually move the needle here:

• Deepinder Goyal (zomato): 
he shares raw notes on culture, recruitment, and product nuance. 
when he posts a hiring call on his personal profile, it gets 100x the high-quality applications that a standard job portal ever would. 
he is the distribution engine for talent.

• Kunal Shah (cred): 
love him or hate him, he is a master of "provocative hooks." he doesn’t sell credit card bills; he sells curiosity. 
he uses his personal profile to build an intellectual monopoly. 
people join cred because they want to be part of the "system" he talks about.

• Nithin & Nikhil Kamath (zerodha): 
i notice they almost never talk about "trading." 
they talks about creator economy, health, sustainable growth, and the ethics of business, built so much institutional trust that zerodha barely spends on marketing. 

if you aren't using your profile to:

1. challenge the "default" way your industry operates.

2. share the technical nuances that ai can't replicate.

3. build a direct line to your buyers that doesn't depend on an ad budget.

...then you are just becoming a distribution bottleneck.

If you’re still hiring an outsourcing partner to simply “throw bodies” at a problem, you’re buying a horse and buggy in the age of the electric jet.

In 2026, the industry has hit a tipping point: Traditional outsourcing is dead. It has been replaced by a hybrid model where senior talent is force-multiplied by AI-driven automation. At UVIK, we see this as the new standard.

1️⃣ From "Uptime" to "Predictive Resilience"
We used to measure success by how fast we fixed a break. Now, we use AI-driven observability to predict failures before they happen. Self-healing scripts trigger automatically.
The Strategy: Don’t pay for recovery; pay for continuous, interrupted availability.

2️⃣ Killing the "Technical Debt" Tax
AI-assisted code reviews and automated refactoring ensure every line of Python is optimized as it’s written. This prevents the "spaghetti code" that typically plagues long-term offshore projects.
The Pro Tip: Demand a partner that uses AI to audit their own code quality in real-time.

3️⃣ Hyper-Speed Pipelines
CI/CD is no longer just a script; it’s intelligent. AI-enabled pipelines decide which tests are critical based on specific code changes, slashing deployment times by up to 60%.
The Bottom Line: Your competitors are deploying 10x a day while you’re stuck behind a manual QA gate.

💡 My advice:
📌 Audit the Toolchain: Ask: "Which AI-agents are integrated into your DevOps pipeline?" No answer? No contract.

📌 Efficiency over Headcount: If a vendor suggests 10 juniors for a task 2 seniors with AI could handle, walk away.

📌 Automate the Growth: The value isn't in the code; it’s in the automation logic that manages it.

If your partner isn't bringing AI to the table, they aren't bringing a future.

After 200 hours of vibe coding with Claude Code, i've built my own SaaS reporting tool.


It's been a long journey, and it's not the end, just the beginning. The last 8 weeks i've been working Monday to Sunday to ship the first version. It's crazy how much I've learnt about AI while vibe coding (more on that in future posts).

I've always been frustrated by most reporting tools, they were missing the features a SaaS startup actually needs.

The challenges I kept hitting:

1. No MRR (monthly recurring revenue) schedule linked to contracts signed and accounting. No Customers tab to keep records of contracts, invoices sent, and invoices that need to go out.
   
2. B2B SaaS forecasts in annual ARR targets and new logos, not percentages. Founders think "£3m of new ARR this year, 40 new logos to get there." Every tool I tried wanted a fixed amount or a %, and there was also no way to track targets achieved vs targets remaining linkedin to pipeline.
   
3. No proper People & Hiring tab, this one pissed me off the most. Founders want a clear list of positions to hire over the next 3 months with planned salaries, by department. Every tool gave you a single hiring row. No summary of forecasted hires, no department view, none of the small things that make it actually usable.
   
4. Startup budgets are complicated, especially for SaaS/AI. You don't just say "£10k to Sales and Marketing." You budget for specific events you might go to, travel, sales partners in different regions. 
   
So what makes this tool different?

1. It's very niched built for SaaS that deal with annual contracts, deferred income and renewals.
   
2. Customers tab as the single source of truth. Every contract, invoice and deferred income release lives against the customer record and MRR schedule rolls up from there automatically.
   
3. Invoice Schedule that auto-creates future invoices from each contract - annual upfront, monthly, quarterly, ramped, with VAT and payment lag, so the forecast knows exactly when cash lands.
   
4. Forecast built in annual ARR targets and new logos, per product, per region. The engine builds the cohorts, applies churn at renewal, flows it through P&L, BS & CF.
   
5. People & Hiring with role-by-role planning. Position, department, salary, NI, pension, start date. Clear summary of who you're hiring and when, by department.
   
6. Budget items that match how startups actually budget, events, travel, partners by region, each with its own payment terms.

and a lot more stuff and more to add. Anyway attaching a few pictures. I am testing it now and there are still some bugs to fix, will need probably another week or two to make sure everything works properly. Every day will post about what I learned about Vibe Coding with Claude Code.

I am very very impressed by the tool i built in the last 2 months!! Still testing the tool on a startup. Apologies for not having some demo data in the screenshots, will make a better presentation next time! :)

Stay tunned!

Today’s job market is broken in many ways.

1. Actual openings inside companies have reduced drastically.  Only role circulations are happening within big organisations .  
2. Senior and executive-level roles are getting impacted the most.  
3. Companies are heavily investing in AI and expecting employees to become “jack of all trades.”  That doesn’t align well with career goals & growth.  
4. Many companies are replacing full-time positions with consultants and contract-based hiring because contractors can be removed anytime without long-term commitments.  
5. The problem is that many of these roles are offering salaries below the candidate’s current market worth.  
6. Your worth should not go backwards. If you are switching jobs, the offer should reflect growth, not a pay cut from your existing base salary.  
7. After 3–4 rounds of interviews, many candidates get rejected in HR discussions simply because companies cannot match their current compensation.  
8. Certifications alone won’t save careers anymore. Real-time experience, execution ability, adaptability, and problem-solving matter far more today.  
9. Keep yourself updated. Learn AI, automation, and modern tools. Also help deserving candidates by sharing genuine knowledge and training.

This market is testing patience, confidence, and adaptability more than technical skills alone.

The people who continuously learn, build, adapt, and refuse to undervalue themselves will eventually come out stronger.

The doors to law just swung wide open in India.⚖️

The Bar Council of India has withdrawn its moratorium on new law colleges — and the ripple effects are already being felt.

And if you're a high schooler even remotely curious about a career in law, this changes your timeline.

Think about what's actually shifting:

→ More law colleges = more seats = less cutthroat competition to get in.
→ A surge in law graduates = a surge in internship demand from firms needing fresh talent.
→ New colleges need to prove themselves = they will chase students, not the other way around.

Now, you might be thinking — "Law is boring. That's not for me."

Fair point. Except law in 2026 looks nothing like your dad's courtroom drama. AI regulation, startup contracts, gaming laws, crypto compliance — every hot industry right now is desperately hiring people who understand both tech and the law.

That's a gap. And gaps are opportunities.

The BCI just created more entry points into one of India's most stable and high-paying professions. The question isn't whether law is a good career. The question is whether you'll research it before everyone else does.

Because by the time it becomes "obvious," the seats will already be taken.

💬 Drop a comment: Are you considering law as a career path? What's stopping you — or what pushed you toward it?

How to Dominate the Sydney Tech Recruitment Market 101… when moving over from the UK

Moving to Sydney is one thing. Owning your market here is another.
Here's what I'd tell every UK tech recruiter landing in Australia:

1. Niche down immediately.
Australia needs 312,000 additional tech workers by 2030, but only produces 7,000 IT graduates a year. 

The gap is enormous. The richest opportunities are in cybersecurity, AI, and cloud:
- 54% of Australian cyber teams are understaffed
- 58% have unfilled positions right now
- 7 of the 10 fastest growing tech roles are AI related

Pick your niche and own it, don’t be a generalist.

2. Speed is your competitive advantage.
The hiring process in the UK taught you rigour, so use what you’ve learnt. 

Sydney's best candidates are off the market in days. Three interview stages max. Fast feedback. No delays. The recruiters winning here are the ones moving the fastest.

3. Build your brand before you land and start posting on LinkedIn before you arrive. 

Sydney is a relationship market. Companies are paying 19% salary premiums and still can't fill roles, but they hire through people they trust. 

Be visible, be credible, be consistent.

4. Don't overlook contracting.
The contractor market in Sydney is mature and lucrative. With companies spending heavily on transformation projects, contract desks are printing right now. If you only focus on perm, you're missing serious opportunity. 

5. Your UK network is an asset, not a liability.
72% of Australian employers actively source talent internationally. 

You're not starting from zero, you're starting with a pipeline.

The market rewards the prepared.

The AI agent just made a $50,000 mistake.

Confident. Convincing. Completely wrong.

So here's the question I keep coming back to:
When an AI agent gets it wrong — who's actually to blame?
1️⃣ The person who built the agent 
2️⃣ The person who used it and trusted the output 
3️⃣ No one — we should accept that AI agents make mistakes
4️⃣ Someone else — tell me in the comments 👇

I have my answer. But I'm genuinely curious where you land — because how we answer this is going to shape every AI policy, SLA contract, and hiring decision for the next decade.

Option 1, 2, 3, or 4? Leave your comments 👇

A founder sent me a line from ChatGPT last week. "Your agency margins should be 40%."

We'd just finished a plan that gets him to 50%+.

The plan is built on an economic model we've been working on together for the last year. It's not the average agency P&L.

Founders are quoting ChatGPT margins back to me as if they were legitimate benchmarks. The number is wrong every time.

The model averages across agencies that haven't done the work. Founders reach for it expecting a stretch number, but they actually get a comfort number.

ChatGPT gave the founder a ceiling, and the founder read it as a target. That's the part that should worry you.

What I'm seeing right now is founders making real decisions against benchmarks the model pulled almost out of thin air.

Hiring decisions. Spend decisions. Growth targets. All sized to a number that thinks smaller than the agency is capable of.

ChatGPT can read your P&L. The economic model behind it lives in your head, your contracts, and what you're building this thing into.

AI is going to keep eating the bottom of the finance stack. The judgement layer is the only part that doesn't average.

A US regional bank just had its CEO's AI clone deliver the prepared remarks on a public earnings call.

Most of the takes will be about the stunt. The actual story is in the contract underneath it.

Customers Bank ($25.9B in assets) signed a multi-year deal with OpenAI to embed engineers inside the bank. AI already writes half their code. They've saved ~28,000 hours — roughly 15 FTEs they didn't have to hire. The CEO is now openly tying AI to revenue-per-employee, not just "productivity."

Three things worth noting if you're a CTO or operator watching this:
1️⃣ The clone is the marketing. The org redesign is the product. Most enterprises will copy the clone and miss the rebuild.
2️⃣ Regulated industries (banking, pharma, process manufacturing) move on this in the same order every time — automate code, then internal ops, then customer-facing. Customers Bank is at step 2. Manufacturing is mostly still at step 1.
3️⃣ "Revenue per employee" is the metric quietly replacing "headcount growth" in board decks. If your 2026 plan still leads with hiring, it's already an old plan.

The interesting question isn't whether your CEO should clone themselves. It's whether your operating model is built for a world where 15% of your work gets done by agents you haven't hired yet.

Is your AI tool supporting recruitment or deciding who to hire? 

The ICO's Recruitment Rewired report suggests it's probably making the decisions. Most employers the ICO engaged with get this wrong.

If the AI tool decides who to hire without "meaningful human involvement" – for example, you approve the tool's decision with a click – this is automated decision-making (ADM) for the purposes of the UK GDPR. As a result, the employer has some meaty compliance tasks to complete to make this lawful.

Three things to know:
1) You could use ADM in recruitment.
2) You can't rely on consent or contract for it.
3) You need to satisfy yourself that the AI provider is responsible.

You can read the rest in the blog and tell me, if you have used such tools, are they useful, do you like them; as a candidate, do you trust them?

And if you sell the AI tool, what questions do you get?

Hire-Into-Clarity: the framework that stops trades owners adding headcount and wondering why they're still drowning.

Most owners think the problem is capacity. They're working 60-hour weeks, the phone won't stop, jobs are slipping through, and the obvious answer feels like another pair of hands. So they hire. And six months later they're working 65-hour weeks, managing someone they didn't have time to train, and the admin has quietly metastasised.

Here's what's actually happening.

A new £28k employee doesn't just cost £28k. Their existence creates roughly £12k of new management overhead through scheduling, payroll admin, HR conversations, performance reviews, and the invisible tax of just being responsible for another human being. You haven't bought capacity. You've bought complexity.

Hire-Into-Clarity says: automate the function first, then hire into a role that has already been simplified.

Step one: map what's actually eating your time. Not what feels busy. What generates the most repetitive decisions and tasks per week. For most trades businesses that's lead response, booking, job updates, and invoice chasing. Write it down.

Step two: automate the repeatable layer. AI can handle first-contact responses, appointment reminders, follow-up sequences, and basic job status updates. This isn't theory. A plumbing outfit in the Midlands running around £600k revenue cut their admin load by roughly 11 hours a week across owner and one admin, before they hired anyone new.

Step three: look at what's left. What remains after automation is typically the work that genuinely requires judgement, relationship, or physical presence. That's the role you hire for. You're not hiring to escape chaos. You're hiring into a clearly scoped, already-leaner operation.

Step four: write the job around what's left, not around what hurts. Most job ads in the trades are just a list of frustrations dressed up as a role description. Hire-Into-Clarity forces you to define the actual decision rights and daily tasks before you post anything.

Step five: measure the admin multiplier before you sign the contract. Ask yourself honestly, how many hours per week will managing this person create? If you can't answer that, you're not ready to hire.

The reason most owners miss this is emotional, not logical. Hiring feels like action. It's tangible. You can point to it. Automation feels like a project you'll get round to eventually. But eventually costs you the margin you needed to afford the hire in the first place.

The businesses I've seen scale cleanly from £400k to £1M aren't the ones who hired fastest. They're the ones who got clear first.

What's the one function in your business that you know could be automated, but you haven't touched yet?

Most people are celebrating this as just another big IT deal.

They’re missing the real shift.


---

Infosys just signed its largest GCC deal

~$500M+ engagement

4,500 employees

Built for Truist Financial

5-year Build-Operate-Transfer model


Sounds impressive.

But that’s not the story.


---

What’s actually happening here

This is not outsourcing.

This is temporary outsourcing to eliminate outsourcing later.

👉 Infosys builds the entire capability
👉 Runs it
👉 Then hands it over

After that?

The client doesn’t need Infosys the same way anymore.


---

This changes the IT services model

Old model: → “We’ll do the work for you”

New model: → “We’ll build your internal engine… then exit”

That’s a completely different business.


---

Why companies want this

More control

Lower long-term cost

Stronger in-house capability

Less vendor dependency


Basically:

They don’t want vendors.

They want self-sufficiency.


---

The part no one talks about

This trend is a double-edged sword.

Short term: → Massive deal sizes
→ Hiring surge

Long term: → Reduced dependency on IT vendors
→ Fewer repetitive service contracts
→ Higher demand for niche, high-skill roles only


---

The bigger signal

GCCs are no longer back offices.

They are becoming: → Core operating hubs
→ AI-driven centers
→ Strategic assets


---

Bottom line

This deal is not just big.

It’s directional.

The industry is moving from:

“Outsource everything”
to
“Build internally. Own everything.”


---

If you’re in tech or consulting, this shift matters more than the headline number.

59% of hiring managers now suspect candidates are using AI fraud in interviews. The trust contract that makes hiring work is already broken.

Here's why this matters more than anyone in recruiting wants to admit:

KORE1 reported on April 20, 2026 that AI has fundamentally rewired tech recruiting on both sides of the table. Candidates prep with LLMs. Recruiters source through AI-augmented tools. And screening fraud has quietly tripled.

But here's the number that should terrify you:

A CBS News investigation found roughly 50% of employers have encountered AI-driven deepfake fraud by early 2026. The FBI has documented 300+ US companies that unknowingly hired North Korean operatives through remote IT worker schemes using stolen identities.

Let's connect the dots:

-> The Fraud Epidemic No One Planned For:
- 59% of hiring managers suspect AI-assisted candidate fraud
- 1 in 3 have caught a fake identity or proxy in an actual interview
- Deepfake screening fraud has tripled year over year
- Nation-state actors are exploiting remote hiring pipelines at scale

-> The Industry Self-Own:
- Recruiting spent a decade eliminating human touchpoints
- Every optimization for speed created a wider attack surface
- The same AI tools that "streamlined" hiring now power the fraud
- Detection technology alone will not fix a process built on disappearing trust

This is what I keep telling people: the real disruption is not AI making recruiting faster. It is AI fraud forcing the entire industry back toward high-touch, relationship-driven hiring that staffing firms abandoned years ago in the name of efficiency.

We optimized ourselves into a trap. The companies that reduced hiring to automated pipelines and asynchronous screens built the exact infrastructure bad actors needed.

No amount of deepfake detection software fixes a process where humans have been engineered out of the loop.

If 59% of hiring managers already assume candidates might be frauds, haven't we already destroyed the foundation that makes hiring work, and isn't more AI detection just treating the symptom?

P.S. The companies quietly winning right now are the ones adding humans back into the process. The most counter-trend move in 2026 hiring is a live, in-person conversation. Think about that.

📊 Hiring Trends in 2026: What's Changing (And What You Need to Know)

The recruitment landscape is shifting fast. Here's what's happening right now:

1. AI Adoption Surge – 75% of companies now use AI in some form of recruitment. Those not using it? They're falling behind.

2. Remote Work Dominance – 60% of open roles are fully remote or hybrid. The talent pool just got exponentially larger.

3. Skills-Based Hiring – Companies are moving away from degree requirements. What you can do matters more than where you studied.

4. Emerging Tech Demand – AI/ML, Cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP), Cybersecurity, and DevOps roles are seeing 40%+ growth year-over-year.

5. Retention Crisis – Hiring is only half the battle. Companies investing in employee experience and growth are winning the talent war.

6. Gig & Contract Work – Flexible staffing models are becoming mainstream. Permanent roles aren't the only option anymore.

The bottom line? Companies that adapt to these trends will attract top talent. Those that don't will struggle to fill critical roles.

Ready to future-proof your hiring strategy? Let's talk.

The Indian IT landscape is witnessing a significant start to the week. After a sharp sell-off on Friday that impacted market valuations, Monday is showing signs of a relief rally, though long-term structural questions persist.
Key updates for today:
Market Performance
The Nifty IT index has rebounded nearly 2% today, led by gains in Infosys and Wipro, recovering from a 5% decline on Friday. Despite this recovery, the sector remains the year’s most challenged performer, down approximately 25% year-to-date.
The Impact of AI
Industry leadership is increasingly acknowledging a structural shift. While Generative AI is causing an estimated 2-3% annual revenue deflation in traditional effort-based services, new AI-native deals are emerging. Wipro’s recent partnership with Kongsberg Digital for AI-powered digital twins highlights this pivot toward high-value technology integration.
Hiring and Talent Trends
The era of volume-based campus hiring is cooling, with active tech job openings down 8% month-on-month. Conversely, demand for specialized roles such as AI Architects and Cybersecurity Specialists has increased significantly, with firms prioritizing niche skills over general scale.
Strategic Outlook
While near-term volatility remains due to global macroeconomic headwinds and conservative revenue guidance, the industry is shifting focus toward Global Capability Centers (GCCs) and outcome-driven contracts.
The Bottom Line: Is this a temporary sentiment correction, or the beginning of a permanent decoupling of revenue from headcount?

The most important shift in enterprise AI right now isn’t better prompts.

It’s something much bigger.

✈️ Airlines don’t rely on one pilot doing everything.

They rely on coordinated teams.

That thought came to mind recently when I attended an Agentic AI & Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) training at Singapore Management University.

It sparked a realization.

Most companies today are trying to build smarter chatbots.

But the real shift may be something entirely different.

It’s the idea of a Transactive Memory System.

In organizations, intelligence is distributed.

• Finance knows the numbers  
• Legal knows compliance  
• HR knows people  
• Operations knows execution  

No one knows everything.

But the organization knows who knows what.

Now look at where AI is going.

Most enterprise AI today looks like this:

User → Chatbot → LLM → Answer

One AI trying to know everything.

But Agentic AI is starting to mirror how real organizations work.

Instead of one AI, we now have specialized agents:

• Finance Agent → budgets & forecasting  
• Legal Agent → contracts & compliance  
• HR Agent → hiring & policies  
• Operations Agent → logistics & supply chain  

And an AI orchestrator coordinating them.

When a question arrives, the orchestrator consults the relevant agents — just like a leadership team meeting.

Finance checks the numbers.  
Legal checks regulations.  
HR checks talent availability.  
Operations checks execution feasibility.

The system then synthesizes the answer.

Not a chatbot.

But something closer to a digital organization.

The companies that win with AI won’t deploy better chatbots.

They will build AI systems that mirror how organizations actually think.

And once that happens, the real question becomes:

If your company had a digital leadership team of AI agents today…

What decision would you ask them to make first?

#AgenticAI #EnterpriseAI #FutureOfWork #AITransformation #SMU #AviationInsightsByFrederick

The Physics of Bureaucracy: Friction

In physics, friction is the force that resists motion between surfaces.

Without friction, movement becomes unstable.
Too much friction, and movement becomes nearly impossible.

Governance works much the same way.

Every reform, innovation, or new idea must move through existing laws, systems, and definitions.
And that is where friction begins.

Technology often moves faster than governance can catch up.

Take ride sharing.
When India’s Motor Vehicles Act was drafted in 1988, policymakers defined transport through the lens of that era. A contract carriage was imagined as one passenger, or one defined group, hiring one vehicle for a specific journey.
Logical for its time.

But 1988 could not fully imagine a world transformed by smartphones, GPS, cheap mobile internet, and real-time route matching.
Today, technology makes something entirely new possible:
Multiple strangers, travelling in the same direction, sharing one cab.

It is efficient.
It lowers the cost for passengers.
It can increase driver earnings.
It reduces congestion.
It can reduce environmental burden.

By modern logic, it solves multiple problems at once.
But governance does not always operate on emerging logic.
It operates on existing definitions.
And when old definitions meet new possibilities, friction appears.
The law remains anchored in the assumptions of the past.

Technology creates opportunities in the present.
Citizens demand solutions for the future.
This is not necessarily bad policymaking.

It is structural friction:
When systems built for one era resist movement into another.
And this is where bureaucracy often becomes misunderstood.
The challenge is not always opposition to progress.

Sometimes, progress is simply moving faster than the legal framework designed to govern it.
So innovation slows.
Policy debates begin.
Regulators intervene.
And what seems obviously beneficial can still become illegal.
This is friction.

The gap between what is now possible… and what systems were originally designed to permit.
Which leads to the bigger question:
When progress is held back by structures built for yesterday, how does society move forward?

In physics, friction alone does not stop change forever.
But overcoming it requires something powerful enough to move what has remained still.

That force begins with inertia.
And in governance, inertia may be even harder to overcome than friction itself.

About the Role

Hiring Compliance and Regulatory Attorneys to design realistic regulatory scenarios, evaluate model outputs against established legal and compliance standards, and help shape how the next generation of AI reasons about regulatory frameworks, internal investigations, and high-stakes compliance work.

We welcome regulatory counsel, in-house compliance attorneys (with JD), former regulators, healthcare and financial regulatory specialists, and senior associates in compliance practice. Specialists in AML/BSA, FCPA, FDA, healthcare regulatory, sanctions, or privacy are all encouraged.

What You'll Do

Design realistic regulatory scenarios drawn from your practice (AML/SAR preparation, OFAC screening, FCPA assessments, FDA submissions, HIPAA reviews, environmental permitting, administrative hearings, privacy/PIA)
Write "golden" reference responses at attorney-level quality
Grade AI-generated responses against structured rubrics
Provide written feedback the research team uses to improve model behavior
Participate in onboarding office hours and specialty calibration sessions

You're a Good Fit If You

Have 1–4 years of professional experience in regulatory or compliance law at a law firm, in-house legal department, government agency, or regulator
Hold a J.D. (U.S./Canada) or LL.B./LL.M. (U.K.) from an accredited law school
Are licensed to practice law in the U.S., Canada, or U.K. (active or inactive)
Have strong experience with one or more of: AML/BSA, OFAC sanctions screening, FCPA/anti-corruption, healthcare regulatory (HIPAA, Stark Law, Anti-Kickback Statute, Medicare/Medicaid), FDA submissions, privacy (GDPR/CCPA/PIA/DPIA), environmental permitting, export control, or administrative hearings
Demonstrate excellent written communication skills with high attention to detail
Bonus Qualifications
CIPP, CFE, CAMS, or similar certification
Internal compliance investigation experience
Experience drafting compliance training programs
Familiarity with regulatory portals (FDA, federalregister.gov, NLRB.gov, EEOC)
Role Highlights
Minimum 20 hours per week (ideally 40+)
Fully remote — must be based in the United States, Canada, or United Kingdom
Role starts immediately, applications reviewed on a rolling basis

🚀 We’re Hiring: Data Engineer (Azure Databricks | PySpark | Cloud)
Looking for an exciting opportunity to work on modern data platforms? We are hiring a hands-on Data Engineer with strong expertise in Databricks, Spark, and Cloud technologies to build scalable and high-performance data pipelines.
🔍 Role Overview
You will design and develop reliable ELT/ETL pipelines, work with Delta Lake, and optimize data workflows for performance and cost while enabling advanced analytics and AI/ML use cases.
📍 Location: Any HM Location
💼 Experience: 5+ Years

📅 Contract Duration: 1 Year
🏢 Work Mode: 4 Days Office | 1 Day WFH
🛠️ Key Responsibilities
• Build and optimize scalable pipelines using Azure Databricks & PySpark
• Develop Delta Lake tables and reusable data components
• Translate business requirements into production-ready ETL/ELT jobs
• Optimize Spark workloads for performance and cost efficiency
• Implement best practices with Unity Catalog and data governance
• Orchestrate workflows and support AI/ML data pipelines
• Collaborate with cross-functional teams and mentor junior engineers
💡 Core Skills Required
✔ Azure Databricks, PySpark, Python, SQL
✔ Strong experience with AWS & Azure Cloud
✔ Databricks Lakehouse, Workflows, SQL, dbt
✔ Terraform / CloudFormation (Infrastructure as Code)
✔ Data warehousing & Lakehouse architecture
⭐ Nice to Have
• Experience with AI/ML pipelines
• Strong problem-solving and performance tuning skills
👉 Interested candidates can share their profiles to rohith.m@codinglimits.com 
or tag someone who fits this role!
